explanation: The Start-up Mode, represented by prop_0, must occur only after all of the following are true together: prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4. This means that in every moment before all these are true, Start-up Mode cannot yet be initiated. Once all are true, it is possible for prop_0 to be true, expressing initiation of Start-up Mode. The LTL formula states that it is always the case that if prop_0 becomes true (Start-up Mode initiated), then previously (or at the same time), all of prop_1 (prop_2), prop_3, and prop_4 were true.

The requirement is that "Start-up Mode" (prop_0) is initiated (becomes true) once prop_1 (connected), air supply, and power source are all attached. Since we only have the three atomic propositions prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2, we assume that prop_1 is the connection to the patient, prop_2 represents both air supply and power source. The statement can be formalized as: whenever prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then eventually prop_0 must hold. We express this as: Globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 become true, then eventually prop_0 must become true.

This requirement specifies that the atomic proposition prop_0 (representing the Start-up Mode initiation) is only allowed to occur after (and not before) prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 have all occurred. In LTL, this is captured by stating that until all of these have become true, prop_0 must not occur. Additionally, once all have occurred, it is required that eventually prop_0 will occur (initiate Start-up Mode).

The requirement says that Start-up Mode (prop_0) should be initiated once (eventually after) prop_1 (and prop_2), prop_3, and prop_4 have occurred (i.e., all are true). In LTL, this is often encoded as: if (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) holds, then prop_0 must eventually occur afterwards. This is classically captured as G((prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) -> F prop_0).

The requirement is to have prop_0 triggered by prop_1 once all of prop_2 (prop_3), prop_4, and prop_5 have occurred. That is, it should be the case that, when all these propositions hold, then (possibly from then on) prop_1 causes prop_0. This is typically captured by stating that after all of prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 have occurred (i.e., eventually all are true), prop_1 triggers prop_0. In LTL, this can be captured by a global implication: whenever all the preconditions are met, then prop_1 implies prop_0.

We interpret the requirement as: Whenever prop_1 ("connection to the patient"), prop_2 ("air supply"), and prop_3 ("power source") all hold (i.e., are 'present'), it should eventually be the case that prop_0 ("Start-up Mode initiated") happens. Since the triggering condition is 'once all connections are attached', we encode that globally, whenever they all become true, it is eventually the case that prop_0 is true.

The property says that prop_0 is triggered by prop_1 once all of prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 are true. This means that when (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) happens, then after that, prop_1 will cause prop_0. One reasonable way to encode this is: whenever (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) happens, then globally after this point, any time prop_1 is true, prop_0 is also true. Before then, there is no such constraint. This can be written as: G(((prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) -> G(prop_1 -> prop_0))).

The requirement is that the Start-up Mode (represented by prop_0) should be initiated only after all of prop_1 (without connection to the patient), prop_2, and prop_3 are present/active. We can represent this as: it is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then previously, all of prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 were true (i.e., prop_0 should not occur before all of these have occurred).

This can be written as: 'It is always the case that (if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 are already true)'.

The meaning is: 'prop_0' should follow 'prop_1' once prop_2 (without connection to the patient), prop_3, and prop_4 have happened. This corresponds to: once all three (prop_2, prop_3, prop_4) have occurred, then prop_1 occurs, and then after that, prop_0 happens. We encode that as: Eventually, all (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) happen; after that, prop_1 happens, and after that prop_0 happens. That means: (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) -> F (prop_1 & F prop_0). To enforce ordering, we need: (1) F(prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) --- eventually all these are true at once, (2) after that, F prop_1, (3) after prop_1, F prop_0. Ordering is chain: so, whenever (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) become true, prop_1 needs to eventually be true after that, and then prop_0. In LTL: G(((prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) & !prop_1) -> F(prop_1 & F prop_0)).

The phrase suggests that prop_1 (e.g. turning on the system) is allowed only after all of prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 (e.g. all connections being attached) have occurred/held. Therefore, it should never be the case that prop_1 happens before all four properties become true. In LTL, this is enforced by stating that 'if not all of prop_2, prop_3, prop_4, and prop_5 are true, then prop_1 must not occur' globally.
